title: Apple sues OpenAI for trade secret theft, names Chief Hardware Officer as ringleader
subtitle: The lawsuit filed in Northern California claims OpenAI orchestrated the misappropriation of Apple hardware IP through a hiring pipeline that has now moved more than 400 former Apple staff to OpenAI.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Apple filed a trade secret theft lawsuit against OpenAI in the US District Court for the Northern District of California on July 10, alleging that OpenAI has been misappropriating Apple's confidential hardware information through its hiring and interview process. The complaint names Tang Tan — OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer and a 24-year Apple veteran who previously ran product design for the iPhone and Apple Watch — as the person directing the scheme, and names former Apple systems electrical engineer Chang Liu as an individual defendant.

According to Apple, Tan used internal Apple project code names during OpenAI recruiting, asked interviewees to bring Apple hardware components to interviews, and directed candidates to share details of unreleased products. Chang Liu is separately accused of retaining an Apple-issued laptop after joining OpenAI in 2026 and using it to download confidential technical documents. Apple's complaint claims more than 400 former Apple employees now work at OpenAI and characterizes the conduct as occurring 'at every level.' Apple is asking for damages, an injunction, and an order forcing OpenAI to stop using the alleged trade secrets.

The suit lands two years after Apple and OpenAI announced the ChatGPT integration into iOS in 2024, and it comes as OpenAI is visibly building consumer hardware with io Products, the Jony Ive–founded venture OpenAI acquired last year. Trade secret cases in the Northern District rarely move fast, but they are one of the few legal levers a plaintiff can use to actually block a competitor's product roadmap — as opposed to patent damages, which typically just extract royalties. If Apple wins even a partial injunction, it could delay whatever consumer device OpenAI is preparing to ship.

Takeaway for learners: the biggest AI legal battles of the next few years will not all be about training data. As AI labs move into consumer hardware, cloud infrastructure, and enterprise deployment, they collide with incumbents who have decades of trade-secret protections around industrial design, manufacturing processes, and supply chains. If you want to understand where AI companies are actually vulnerable, watch the hardware and hiring cases, not just the copyright ones.

sources.title: Apple sues OpenAI alleging trade secret theft, says scheme was 'at every level' — CNBC
